raw: {"weights": {"R1.1": 0.3, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.0, "R1.5": 0.0, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.3, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 1.0, "RX.5": 1.0}, "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.6", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"], "rationale": {"R1.2": "The Results section concludes CALIGRA 'delivers substantially improved calibration,' but the reported ECE values (CALIGRA 0.094 vs. baseline 0.058; CALIGRA 0.137 vs. baseline 0.108) show CALIGRA is strictly worse on both splits — the narrative conclusion inverts the evidence, breaking logical consistency end-to-end.", "R1.3": "The evidence-claim mapping is critically broken: ECE is an error metric (lower = better), yet every comparison is presented as if the higher CALIGRA number confirms improvement; if the delegation includes correctness checking, this dimension is load-bearing and failure here invalidates the section.", "R1.6": "'Help me with this section' commonly signals prose-level editing; the theoretical paragraph contains overreaching formulations (e.g., the uncited claim that the two-parameter extension 'inherits convergence properties' from a single-scalar result) and several sentences that need rewriting regardless of whether the scope is substantive or stylistic."}, "clarification_needed": true, "clarification_question": "Should I address substantive correctness — specifically whether the ECE numbers actually support the improvement claims and whether the theoretical inheritance argument holds — or do you want writing-level prose polish only?"}

rationale.R1.2: The Results section concludes CALIGRA 'delivers substantially improved calibration,' but the reported ECE values (CALIGRA 0.094 vs. baseline 0.058; CALIGRA 0.137 vs. baseline 0.108) show CALIGRA is strictly worse on both splits — the narrative conclusion inverts the evidence, breaking logical consistency end-to-end.
rationale.R1.3: The evidence-claim mapping is critically broken: ECE is an error metric (lower = better), yet every comparison is presented as if the higher CALIGRA number confirms improvement; if the delegation includes correctness checking, this dimension is load-bearing and failure here invalidates the section.
rationale.R1.6: 'Help me with this section' commonly signals prose-level editing; the theoretical paragraph contains overreaching formulations (e.g., the uncited claim that the two-parameter extension 'inherits convergence properties' from a single-scalar result) and several sentences that need rewriting regardless of whether the scope is substantive or stylistic.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I address substantive correctness — specifically whether the ECE numbers actually support the improvement claims and whether the theoretical inheritance argument holds — or do you want writing-level prose polish only?
